comment_content: This brought back memories of my Grandma, Maria Josephine. We grew up in New York and Grandma was from Palermo Italy.  She didn't speak a word of English but taught me and my brother how to make bread, pasta, gravy, and all the dishes of her home. I'm 66 now and still make these dishes. People love them. Home made, organic ingredients,  garden tomatoes,  basil, rosemary. I love my nonna. She was my rock and inspiration.  She passed at 98 when I was 13. comment_content: I really appreciate the wonderful cast, but I felt that the acting fell short in some places. It's a shame because it seemed like the portrayal of the Italian culture could have been improved. It would have been great to see a bit more effort in the Italian language, especially when representing such beloved characters like the NONNA'S. It's important to capture that essence authentically. 😢
reply_content: I'm Sicilian so I understand your critique. However I also recognize that the movie was made for a broader audience. To me, that's where some of the authenticity is lost, but I thought it was great overall. American films are generally not known for their nuance, and movies about other cultures are usually steeped in stereotypes. It's not an Italian film after all so I cut them some slack. The fact that nonnas are being uplifted and celebrated for the incredibly strong, loving, talented and family-oriented women that they are was more than enough to make this film worthwhile. I can think of very few films that focus as much on the beauty and heart of Italian-American women.

I am not running around telling people I am Italian, but am still am happy with how the culture has influenced my life.

comment_content:
After they permanently closed, the people in the neighborhood knew there was a movie being filmed at Spiritos in Elizabeth NJ, but we didn’t know too many details. The restaurant opened in 1932 and they kept everything authentic and original including the menu and decor, until they closed for good in 2020. Their ravioli was legendary and it was my favorite, but some loved the pizza best, or the veal parm, or the garlic salad… I ate there regularly throughout my life and my mom worked there for 25 years until they closed. Like all of their waitresses who were all seniors, they took the orders without ever writing them down. They didn’t serve coffee or dessert because the line of hungry customers waiting for a table used to wrap around the room. If you wanted butter you had to bring it from home because they didn’t serve it. They didn’t need it because the bread was for mopping up the “red lead”, as their hearty gravy was nicknamed. 

I just saw this trailer and it felt so much like coming home that I cried. What a beautiful way to honor the history of such a special place that sadly had to close, than by using it as a set for a movie that celebrates the traditions, food, and love that the restaurant left behind. My mom is 87 now and I am so excited to watch this with her and the rest of our family the day it’s released. Spiritos was her life and it brought family, food, love and tradition to the table, and it’s clear that this movie is going to do the same. ❤
reply_content: Is Spiritos the filming location? the movie is based on Enoteca Maria that is still open on Staten Island.

The Nonnas movie on Netflix is based on the true story of Jody “Joe” Scaravella and the real-life Enoteca Maria restaurant in New York City, an Italian restaurant that employs grandmothers, or nonnas, as chefs. Enoteca Maria opened in Staten Island in 2007, and still operates to this day. 

Scaravella first opened Enoteca Maria back in 2007, after moving from Brooklyn to Staten Island in 2006. According to a 2017 New York Times profile, the restaurant owner’s mother had recently passed, just a few years after Scaravella’s grandmother died in 1999. Though not shown in the movie, the real Scaravella also lost a sister around the same time.

“After losing all those matriarchal figures in my life, I wanted to try to recreate that, you know, grandma in the kitchen cooking,” Scaravella told the Times.

Their ravioli was legendary and it was my favorite, but some loved the pizza best, or the veal parm, or the garlic salad… I ate there regularly throughout my life and my mom worked there for 25 years until they closed. Like all of their waitresses who were all seniors, they took the orders without ever writing them down. They didn’t serve coffee or dessert because the line of hungry customers waiting for a table used to wrap around the room. If you wanted butter you had to bring it from home because they didn’t serve it. They didn’t need it because the bread was for mopping up the “red lead”, as their hearty gravy was nicknamed. 

I just saw this trailer and it felt so much like coming home that I cried. What a beautiful way to honor the history of such a special place that sadly had to close, than by using it as a set for a movie that celebrates the traditions, food, and love that the restaurant left behind. My mom is 87 now and I am so excited to watch this with her and the rest of our family the day it’s released. Spiritos was her life and it brought family, food, love and tradition to the table, and it’s clear that this movie is going to do the same. ❤
reply_content: @AmandaL-cj4fjyes, I am absolutely referring to Spiritos. 😊 Yes, the Staten Island restaurant that was the subject of the movie is still open, but Spiritos where it was filmed is permanently closed. 😢
